behaviors.description: A file placed inside a subdirectory of the watched root is processed, not only files placed directly in the root directory.
behaviors.justification: Behavior evidence: "file placed inside a subdirectory of the watched root is processed." Concrete miss at lower level: an integration test with mocked watcher events can pass even if real recursive OS notifications (e.g., missing newly created nested paths) never fire. Boundary type: real-infra (OS filesystem event delivery). Rejected Integration because the failure depends on real watcher/OS semantics, not only module wiring.
behaviors.test_description: Start the daemon against a real temp directory tree, create a nested subdirectory under the watched root, write a file there, and assert exactly one transformed output is produced in the output directory within timeout.

behaviors.description: The deduplication logic correctly discards a second processing request for the same file path within a configurable time window.
behaviors.justification: Behavior evidence: "deduplication logic ... discards a second processing request ... within a configurable time window." Concrete miss at Unit: a pure function test would miss state handoff bugs between request intake, shared dedupe cache, and pipeline enqueue timing. Boundary type: handoff-state. Rejected System because this behavior text is about duplicate requests and time-window policy, not OS-specific filesystem signaling; it can be reproduced in-process.
behaviors.test_description: Wire real intake + dedupe + enqueue modules with an in-memory clock/test timer control; submit the same file path twice inside the configured window and assert only one pipeline execution, then submit after window expiry and assert a second execution occurs.

behaviors.description: When the operating system emits multiple filesystem events for a single file write, the daemon processes the file exactly once.
behaviors.justification: Behavior evidence: "When the operating system emits multiple filesystem events..." Concrete miss at Integration: mocked single-event or idealized event streams hide real duplicate event bursts/coalescing patterns from OS backends. Boundary type: real-infra. Rejected Integration explicitly because the behavior names OS-emitted event multiplicity as the failure source.
behaviors.test_description: Run daemon with real filesystem watcher, perform a write pattern known to trigger multiple events (create+append+close/atomic rename), capture processing count, and assert exactly one completed transformation/output for that file.

behaviors.description: If the watched directory is removed and then recreated while the daemon is running, the daemon resumes watching the new directory without a restart.
behaviors.justification: Behavior evidence: "watched directory is removed and then recreated while the daemon is running." Concrete miss at Integration: fake watcher callbacks can bypass real loss/rebind behavior when OS watch handles become invalid after deletion. Boundary type: real-infra. Rejected Integration because directory lifecycle and watch reattachment correctness depends on real OS watcher behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Start daemon on a real directory, remove the watched directory, recreate it at the same path, then create a new file and assert it is processed without restarting the process.

behaviors.description: When multiple files arrive at the same time, they are processed in the order determined by their creation timestamps.
behaviors.justification: Behavior evidence: "multiple files arrive at the same time" and ordering by "creation timestamps." Concrete miss at Integration: in-process stubs can impose perfect timestamp precision/order, missing real filesystem timestamp granularity/tie behavior and actual event arrival races. Boundary type: real-infra. Rejected Integration because ordering correctness here is coupled to real file metadata semantics and concurrent arrival behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Create several files nearly simultaneously on real filesystem (including deliberate close timing), record observed processing order from daemon logs/hooks, and assert order matches creation-time policy (with defined tie-break rule if timestamps equal).

behaviors.description: A file that is still being written to when the arrival event fires is not picked up for processing until writing is complete.
behaviors.justification: Behavior evidence: "still being written to when the arrival event fires" and defer processing "until writing is complete." Concrete miss at Integration: mocked writes/events cannot faithfully reproduce partial-write visibility, file-handle/flush timing, and rename/close semantics that cause premature reads in production. Boundary type: real-infra. Rejected Integration because completion detection is inherently OS/filesystem timing dependent.
behaviors.test_description: With daemon running, open a real file for incremental writes, trigger arrival visibility before final close, verify daemon does not process intermediate content, close/finish write, then assert processing occurs once with full final content.
